chapter_context.summary: Eliphaz starts the second cycle, accusing Job of undermining piety, and detailing the terrifying ruin, anxiety, and brief prosperity of the wicked.

outline.text: Eliphaz Accuses Job of Presumption and Undermining Religion
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Eliphaz Accuses Job of Presumption and Undermining Religion (15:1-16), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Job.

outline.text: Eliphaz Portrays the Terrors and Short-Lived Prosperity of the Wicked
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Eliphaz Portrays the Terrors and Short-Lived Prosperity of the Wicked (15:17-35), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Job.

key_themes.title: The Mystery of Suffering
key_themes.description: Job demonstrates that suffering is not always a direct consequence of personal sin, challenging simplistic retributive theology.

key_themes.title: Divine Sovereignty
key_themes.description: God's answer from the whirlwind exalts His absolute power, wisdom, and control over all creation, far exceeding human understanding.

devotional.question: Reflecting on the events of Chapter 15, how can you remain trusting in God when circumstances are incomprehensible and painful?
How does this chapter prompt you to surrender your demands for explanation and trust in the absolute wisdom of the Creator?

cultural_historical.title: The Land of Uz and Wisdom Literature
cultural_historical.text: Job is set in the land of Uz and belongs to the Wisdom Literature genre of the Hebrew Bible, exploring profound philosophical and theological questions.
